does: This paper introduces Strata-Sword, a hierarchical safety evaluation benchmark for large language models and large reasoning models that categorizes jailbreak instructions by reasoning complexity. Rather than treating all adversarial prompts equally, the framework stratifies attacks into complexity levels, from simple instructions reflecting basic value alignment to complex multi-step reasoning attacks that probe deeper safety vulnerabilities. The benchmark evaluates both traditional LLMs and reasoning-enhanced models (LRMs) across these complexity tiers, measuring how model safety degrades with increasingly sophisticated attack instructions. This hierarchical approach reveals that different complexity levels expose distinct failure modes and safety capabilities in language models.
connects: This work extends research on adversarial robustness and jailbreak attacks against LLMs by moving beyond flat evaluation methods that either focus solely on content-level harms or aggregate diverse attack types without distinguishing their difficulty. It builds on Chain-of-Thought reasoning literature and emerging work on large reasoning models, recognizing that enhanced reasoning capabilities create both opportunities and new vulnerabilities. The paper responds to limitations in existing safety benchmarks like those evaluating alignment and interpretability, which lack principled categorization of attack complexity. It connects to complexity-aware evaluation frameworks in adversarial machine learning and builds on the intuition that safety evaluation should mirror human threat modeling by considering both basic and sophisticated attacks.
enables: This hierarchical evaluation framework enables more nuanced understanding of where models fail under adversarial pressure, allowing researchers to target safety improvements at specific complexity levels. It supports development of complexity-aware defense mechanisms that address simple jailbreaks differently from sophisticated multi-step reasoning attacks. The benchmark facilitates better safety comparisons between traditional LLMs and emerging reasoning models, informing deployment decisions in high-stakes domains. Future work can use Strata-Sword to study whether safety improvements at one complexity level transfer to others, develop targeted training methods for complex reasoning safety, and investigate how scaling affects vulnerability across attack complexity tiers. The framework provides a foundation for establishing safety standards proportional to the sophistication of potential attacks.
